fact: December 30 is exactly one day before the last day of the year, making it the second-to-last day on Earth's calendar!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls in the middle of winter when many animals are fast asleep in hibernation.
The sun sets super early on December 30 in places like Alaska - sometimes before 4 PM, leaving kids in darkness for most of the day!
December 30 always falls under the Capricorn zodiac sign, represented by a half-goat, half-fish creature that loves climbing mountains.
This date sits just 9 days after the winter solstice, meaning daylight is still incredibly short in northern countries.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, December 30 is blazing hot summer weather - perfect for beach days and ice cream!
Many evergreen trees are often still covered in thick snow on this date in northern climates, creating magical winter wonderlands that look like frosting on giant green cakes.
December 30 marks when many nocturnal animals like owls and bats are most active, since the long winter nights give them extra hunting time.
The moon phase on December 30 changes every year, but when it's full, the bright moonlight reflects off snow and makes everything glow like a giant nightlight.
In really cold places, your breath creates instant fog clouds on December 30 because the air temperature is so much colder than your warm body.
This date typically falls during the time when many fish swim deeper underwater to escape freezing surface temperatures in lakes and ponds.
December 30 happens when winter winds are often at their strongest in northern regions, creating howling sounds that can rattle windows and blow snow into crazy spiral shapes.
